questions.question: What does 'recovery' from bipolar disorder realistically look like?

options.text: Never experiencing another mood episode again
Longer stable periods, shorter and less severe episodes, and faster recognition of warning signs
Being able to stop all medication and therapy
Feeling happy all the time without mood fluctuations

questions.explanation: Recovery from bipolar disorder doesn't mean never having another episode. It means longer stable periods between episodes, shorter and less severe episodes when they occur, faster recognition of early warning signs, and less disruption to daily life. The goal is progress, not perfection.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an 'If/Then' action plan in relapse prevention?

options.text: To predict exactly when episodes will occur
To eliminate all early warning signs
To have pre-decided actions for specific warning signs so you don't have to make decisions during a vulnerable moment
To prove to others that you are managing your condition

questions.explanation: An If/Then action plan pairs specific warning signs with specific actions (e.g., 'If I sleep less than 5 hours for 2 nights, I will contact my prescriber within 24 hours'). This removes the need to make decisions during vulnerable moments when judgment may be impaired, ensuring you respond quickly and effectively.

questions.question: When should you create a crisis plan?

options.text: Only after a crisis has already occurred
During a manic episode when you feel confident
While you are stable, so that decisions are already made before a crisis occurs
Crisis plans are unnecessary if you take medication

questions.explanation: A crisis plan should be created while you're stable so that important decisions—who to call, where to go, what steps to take—are already made before a crisis occurs. During a crisis, judgment and decision-making may be impaired, making a pre-made plan essential for safety.

questions.question: If you experience a relapse despite following your prevention plan, what does this mean?

options.text: You have failed and your plan is worthless
Your medication has stopped working permanently
You have a chronic medical condition that sometimes flares up—relapse is information, not failure
You should stop all treatment and give up

questions.explanation: Relapse does not equal failure. Bipolar disorder is a chronic condition that can flare up despite your best efforts. Each episode provides information to update and improve your prevention plan. After an episode, be gentle with yourself, debrief with your treatment team, update your plan, and gradually resume your routines.

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the 'four pillars' of lifestyle maintenance for long-term bipolar stability?

options.text: Working as many hours as possible to stay busy
Consistent sleep, routine and social rhythm, stress management, and substance avoidance
Avoiding all social interaction to reduce stress
Only focusing on medication and ignoring lifestyle factors

questions.explanation: The four pillars of lifestyle maintenance for bipolar stability are: (1) consistent sleep with regular sleep/wake times, (2) routine and social rhythm with stable daily structure, (3) stress management with coping plans and boundaries, and (4) substance avoidance since alcohol, drugs, and excess caffeine destabilize mood and interfere with medication.

title: Relapse Prevention and Long-Term Stability - Quiz
